_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: She traded her mother country's coins for her own name and bet it all on rocks in the ground.

museum_objects.title: C-Day Decimal Currency Launch Materials
museum_objects.description: Original promotional and educational materials from 'C-Day,' when Australia switched from pounds to decimal dollars.
museum_objects.significance: Australia gave up its British pounds and shillings for good on this day — Scarlett's very first act was declaring independence from her mother country's math.
museum_objects.plain_language: Original materials from 1966's 'C-Day,' when Australia switched from British pounds to its own decimal dollar system.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Holey Dollar
museum_objects.description: An early Australian coin created by cutting the center out of a Spanish dollar to prevent hoarding and export.
museum_objects.significance: A century and a half before Scarlett existed, colonial Australia was already improvising its own money out of pure necessity — cutting holes in someone else's currency to make it theirs.
museum_objects.plain_language: An old Australian coin made by literally cutting a hole in the middle of a Spanish coin — an early, improvised form of Australian money.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

display_alternatives.title: Holey dollar coinage NSW 1813 a128577 02.jpg
display_alternatives.attribution: Colonial Government of New South Wales

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: First Polymer Banknote (1988 Bicentennial $10)
museum_objects.description: The world's first polymer banknote, designed by Harry Williamson with technology developed by Australia's CSIRO.
museum_objects.significance: Scarlett didn't just adopt currency technology — she invented it. This note is the reason nearly every country's banknotes today resist counterfeiting the way they do.
museum_objects.plain_language: The world's very first plastic banknote, invented in Australia in 1988 — most countries' money is based on this same technology today.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: eGold Archive: Ballarat/Bendigo Gold Rush Photographs
museum_objects.description: Historical photographs documenting the Victorian gold rush that first made Australia a resource-driven economy.
museum_objects.significance: A century before mining stocks and iron ore exports, Scarlett's national character was already being forged in these dusty goldfields.
museum_objects.plain_language: Old photographs of the 1850s Australian gold rush, which first turned the country's economy into a resource-driven one.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Royal Australian Mint Opening Records
museum_objects.description: Official records of the opening of the Royal Australian Mint in Canberra, established just before decimal currency's launch.
museum_objects.significance: This mint was built specifically to give Scarlett her own physical body — coins struck by Australians, for Australians, for the first time.
museum_objects.plain_language: Official records of the opening of Australia's own mint in 1965, built specifically to make Australia's new decimal coins.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

world_map.map_description: Scarlett was born on Decimal Currency Day in 1966 and grew into a resource-backed currency shaped by gold rushes, iron ore, and the vast mineral wealth of the Australian outback.

nodes.label: Canberra (Royal Australian Mint)
nodes.description: Home of the Royal Australian Mint, established in 1965 to produce the new decimal coinage.

nodes.label: Ballarat / Bendigo Goldfields
nodes.description: Site of the 1850s Victorian gold rush that first established Australia's resource-driven economic identity.

nodes.label: Pilbara Iron Ore Region
nodes.description: One of the world's largest iron ore-producing regions, a modern pillar of the Australian dollar's value.

nodes.label: Kalgoorlie Super Pit
nodes.description: One of Australia's largest gold mines, symbolizing the country's ongoing dependence on mineral wealth.

nodes.label: Sydney (RBA Museum)
nodes.description: Home to the Reserve Bank of Australia's museum documenting the full history of Australian currency.
